title: 👑 Elite Challenge ⭐⭐⭐⭐: Idioms and Figurative Language

questions.prompt:
Read: "The audit revealed that the accounting irregularities were merely the tip of the iceberg. Senior staff had been turning a blind eye to the problem for years."
"Turning a blind eye" (not in the cours) most likely means:

options.text: Deliberately ignoring something they knew about.
Working in poor lighting conditions.
Making a careful examination of the problem.
Reporting the irregularities to management.

questions.explanation: Using context: the irregularities were the tip of the iceberg (small sign of something bigger), and senior staff had been doing something with them "for years" — a negative, passive response. "Turning a blind eye" (a deliberate choice to not look/notice) perfectly fits: ignoring a known problem. (b) is literal; (c) is the opposite; (d) contradicts the idea of hiding the issue.

questions.prompt: Choose the response that correctly explains the figurative device in the sentence: "Time is a thief that steals our youth."

options.text: It is a simile comparing time to a thief using 'like'.
It is a metaphor: time is implicitly identified as a thief, attributing theft (stealing youth) to a non-human concept.
It is an idiom because 'thief' is a fixed expression in English.
It is a literal description of how time can be stolen.

questions.explanation: "Time is a thief" is a classic metaphor: it equates time (abstract) with a thief (concrete) without using like or as, and extends the comparison by attributing the theft of youth. It is not a simile (no like/as), not a fixed idiom, and certainly not literal.

questions.prompt:
Read this paragraph: "The new CEO hit the ground running, immediately going the extra mile to win over sceptical investors. Within months, she had turned the company's fortunes around."
Which idiom is NOT used in this paragraph?

options.text: hit the ground running
going the extra mile
turned the company's fortunes around
hit a brick wall

questions.explanation: "Hit a brick wall" (reach an obstacle that stops progress) does not appear in the paragraph. The paragraph contains: hit the ground running (start with immediate energy), going the extra mile (making extra effort), and turned [something] around (reversed a bad situation). "Hit a brick wall" would contradict the overall positive story.

questions.prompt: Find the sentence where a figurative expression is MISUSED because the form is broken.

options.text: She sees eye to eye with her colleague on most issues.
He let the cat out of the bag before the surprise was ready.
They spilled all of the beans about the new product launch.
It costs an arm and a leg to park in the city centre.

questions.explanation: The error in (c) is "all of the beans" — the fixed form of this idiom is simply "spill the beans" without any modification. Adding "all of" breaks the fixed phrase and is non-native. (a) see eye to eye, (b) let the cat out of the bag, and (d) cost an arm and a leg are all in their correct, unmodified forms.

questions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses TWO different figurative devices (one idiom and one metaphor or simile)?

options.text: The proposal hit a brick wall, and the silence in the room was deafening.
He bit the bullet and he also bit the bullet again.
They see eye to eye and they see eye to eye.
She went the extra mile, going the extra mile every single day.

questions.explanation: "Hit a brick wall" is an idiom (reach an obstacle); "the silence was deafening" is a paradoxical metaphor attributing a loud quality to silence. These are two distinct figurative devices used correctly in one sentence. (b), (c), and (d) repeat the same idiom rather than combining two different devices.

questions.prompt:
Read: "The ambassador's speech was a tightrope walk — one wrong word and the entire alliance could collapse."
Which analysis is MOST accurate?

options.text: "Tightrope walk" is a simile comparing the speech to a physical act.
"Tightrope walk" is a metaphor: the speech is described as a tightrope walk to convey extreme delicacy and the risk of catastrophic failure.
"Tightrope walk" is a fixed idiom with the meaning 'speak slowly and clearly'.
"Collapse" is used literally to describe the alliance's headquarters.

questions.explanation: "A tightrope walk" is used here as a metaphor — the speech (abstract) is identified with the precarious physical act of walking a tightrope to convey the extreme risk and delicacy of the situation. There is no like or as, so it is not a simile (a). It is not a fixed idiom with a single set meaning (c). "Collapse" refers to the breakdown of the alliance, not a literal building (d).
